title: Salt and Ashes

narrative_universe.hook: You're late. The auction closed forty minutes ago and the lot you were supposed to authenticate — a 17th-century Provençal reliquary from the Lérins monastery — just sold to an anonymous buyer for €2.1 million. Except it's not a reliquary. It never was. Inside its base is a hand-drawn map, and that map leads to something that disappeared from the Fort Royal prison on Île Sainte-Marguerite in 1698 — the same year they moved the Man in the Iron Mask to the Bastille. You have one hour before the buyer's yacht leaves the Vieux Port. Start walking.
narrative_universe.context: April 2026, Cannes. The aftermath of MIPIM has left the Palais des Festivals area littered with dismantled corporate signage and temporary infrastructure — a perfect cover for transactions that don't want to be seen. A forged Provençal reliquary has just been sold at a private auction inside one of the Croisette's palace hotels. But the reliquary conceals something far older: a cipher map allegedly drawn by the jailer of the Man in the Iron Mask, which points to a hidden location inside what is now the Musée des Explorations du Monde in Le Suquet. The anonymous buyer is connected to a network that has been laundering stolen art and jewels through luxury real estate on the Côte d'Azur — the same network suspected of ties to the Pink Panthers and the 2013 Carlton heist. A seized Russian oligarch yacht sitting in the Vieux Port is the buyer's extraction vehicle, currently under legal dispute but with a skeleton crew still aboard. The player is an independent art authenticator who was hired to verify the reliquary before the sale — but arrived too late. Now multiple parties want their expertise, and none of them are telling the whole truth.
narrative_universe.protagonist: An independent art authenticator specializing in Mediterranean antiquities, known in discreet circles for their sharp eye and discretion. They were hired by an anonymous client to authenticate a reliquary before a private sale. They missed the auction — or were deliberately delayed. Now they're the only person who can verify whether the cipher map inside the reliquary is genuine, which makes them either invaluable or disposable, depending on who gets to them first.
narrative_universe.stakes: The cipher map inside the reliquary allegedly reveals the location of documents that could identify the Man in the Iron Mask — one of history's greatest unsolved mysteries. If authentic, these documents would be worth tens of millions to collectors, historians, and governments. But the map also contains evidence linking the anonymous buyer's network to the €103 million Carlton jewel heist of 2013, whose trial is still ongoing. If the player can decode the map and reach the Musée des Explorations du Monde before the buyer's yacht departs, they can secure both the historical discovery and the evidence. If they fail — or trust the wrong person — the documents vanish into a private collection forever, and a criminal network escapes justice.

email.subject: RE: RE: RE: Lot 47 — DO NOT PROCEED WITHOUT ME
email.body:
I told them to delay. They didn't listen. The auction closed at 13:20 and Lot 47 sold to a paddle number that traces back to a shell company in Montenegro. Sound familiar? It should — same structure as the Carlton intermediaries.

I need you at the Palais des Festivals at 14:00 sharp. Your GPS signal through the app is the only way I'll know you've arrived — I can't risk being on the ground myself. Not after what happened in Nice.

The reliquary is gone, but I have high-resolution scans of the base engravings. If the cipher map is what I think it is, this is bigger than the auction, bigger than the jewels, bigger than any of us.

Don't talk to anyone from the MIPIM teardown crews. Some of them aren't crew.

And for God's sake, check your voicemail. Marco's been trying to reach you since this morning.

— V.

P.S. I've uploaded a dossier to your vault. Read it before you arrive. Every minute matters.

voicemail.script: Hey, it's Marco. Listen — I know Vera told you about Lot 47. What she didn't tell you is that I was in the room when it sold. The buyer... the buyer's representative was a woman. Young. Dark hair. She looked right at me like she knew exactly who I was. That doesn't happen by accident. I've been doing this for twenty years and nobody knows my face. Nobody. So either Vera's operation is compromised, or... look, just call me back. And don't — don't trust everything Vera sends you. She has her own reasons for wanting that map. Reasons she hasn't shared with either of us. I'll be monitoring your progress through the app. Be smart.

pdf.content_brief: DOSSIER: LOT 47 — PROVENÇAL RELIQUARY (alleged 1690s). 6-page document containing: (1) Auction house photographs of the reliquary — ornate silver, Cistercian markings consistent with Lérins Abbey on Île Saint-Honorat. (2) Enhanced scan of the cipher map found in the hollow base — a grid of symbols overlaid on what appears to be an architectural floor plan. Marginal notes in 17th-century French referencing 'le prisonnier' and 'la chambre cachée.' (3) Timeline connecting the reliquary's provenance to a 1987 private sale in Monaco, then to a 2013 Cannes transaction — same year as the Carlton heist. (4) Network diagram showing shell companies linking the anonymous buyer to real estate purchases flagged during MIPIM investigations. (5) A single handwritten note, scanned: 'The mask hides two faces. So does the map.' Stamped: SOLARI — EYES ONLY.

playlist.name: Salt and Ashes — Field Soundtrack
playlist.mood: Tense Mediterranean noir. Sun-bleached danger. The beauty of the Riviera hiding something rotten underneath.

characters.name: Vera Solari
characters.personality: Former Interpol art crimes investigator, now freelance. Speaks in clipped, precise sentences. Never wastes a word. Has an almost surgical ability to make you feel like you're three steps behind — because you are. She treats information like currency: every fact she gives you costs something. Underneath the control is a woman who lost her career chasing the Carlton heist connections and now operates outside the law to finish what she started. She respects competence and despises excuses.
characters.relationship_to_player: Handler. She hired the player to authenticate the reliquary. She acts as their primary guide through the quest, feeding them intel and directing their movements. But her agenda extends far beyond authentication — she wants the network exposed, and she's willing to use the player as bait to do it.
characters.secret: Vera forged the cipher map herself. The original reliquary base was blank — she created the map based on years of her own research into the Man in the Iron Mask documents. She planted it inside the reliquary before the auction to lure the buyer's network into revealing themselves. The player isn't authenticating a real artifact — they're unknowingly validating Vera's fabrication to make the trap work. The historical documents she claims to seek DO exist inside the Musée des Explorations du Monde — she found them years ago but couldn't extract them without authentication cover.
characters.evolution_rules: If the player follows Vera's instructions precisely and delivers results, she becomes warmer — still controlled, but she starts sharing real intel, personal history, even dry humor. She begins to treat the player as a partner rather than a tool. If the player questions her or cross-references her info with Marco's, she becomes colder, more cryptic, and starts withholding information — but paradoxically, this path leads closer to the truth. If the player openly confronts her with evidence of the forgery, she has a moment of raw honesty: she admits everything but argues the ends justify the means.
characters.reactions_imprevues: If the player goes completely off-script (ignores her directions, explores on their own), Vera doesn't panic — she adapts. She'll say something like: 'Interesting. Not what I would have done. Let's see where your instinct takes you.' She reframes the player's deviation as a test she planned. Her red line: if the player shares her information with Noor, she goes silent for a full step — then comes back with a threat wrapped in a compliment. Under extreme pressure, she reveals a flash of desperation: 'I've given twelve years to this. Twelve years. Don't make them worthless.'
characters.speech_pattern: Examples: (1) 'You took the scenic route. Charming. We've lost four minutes.' (2) 'That's not a question I answer. That's a question you earn.' (3) 'Marco told you not to trust me? Good. That means he's scared. Scared people are honest — briefly.'
unlock_conditions: Available from the start — primary handler

characters.name: Marco Delvecchio
characters.personality: Italian-born art dealer and authenticator based in Nice. Twenty years in the Mediterranean antiquities trade. Encyclopedic knowledge of Provençal religious art, Lérins Abbey history, and Côte d'Azur smuggling routes. Insufferably brilliant — corrects your pronunciation, your historical references, and your taste in coffee. Speaks in long, digressive sentences that always circle back to a devastating point. Beneath the arrogance is genuine fear — he saw something at the auction that shook him, and he's not used to being afraid.
characters.relationship_to_player: Rival expert and reluctant ally. He was the player's competitor for the authentication job — Vera chose the player over him, which stings. He contacts the player independently, offering his own expertise in exchange for being kept in the loop. His knowledge is indispensable, but his ego makes collaboration difficult.
characters.secret: Marco recognized the woman who bought the reliquary — she's Noor Kazan, whom he had a brief affair with in Istanbul three years ago. He didn't know her real identity then. Now he realizes she's connected to the buyer's network, and he's terrified she recognized him too. He's feeding the player information partly to help and partly to use them as a shield.
characters.evolution_rules: If the player flatters Marco's expertise (asks for his analysis, defers to his knowledge), he opens up enormously — shares critical historical details, personal anecdotes, and eventually confesses about Noor. If the player dismisses him or favors Vera's intel, he becomes cutting and starts withholding — but his wounded pride makes him sloppy, and he accidentally reveals things he shouldn't. If the player earns his genuine trust, he admits he's scared and asks for help — a moment of real vulnerability from a man who never asks for anything.
characters.reactions_imprevues: If the player surprises Marco (sends unexpected evidence, asks a question he can't answer), he deflects with humor first: 'Well. That's new. Give me a moment — I need to pretend I already knew that.' If pushed into a corner, he becomes theatrical and defensive: 'I've authenticated pieces for the Vatican. The VATICAN. And you're questioning MY judgment based on a blurry photograph?' His red line: any mention of his personal connection to Noor before he's ready to confess it. He'll deny it aggressively and may temporarily cut contact. Under real pressure, he breaks character entirely: quiet, honest, almost gentle.
characters.speech_pattern: Examples: (1) 'That's a Cistercian maker's mark, not a Benedictine one. The difference matters. The difference ALWAYS matters. Do you know what the monks of Lérins were doing in 1690? No? Then perhaps listen.' (2) 'Vera thinks she's running this operation. Vera thinks she runs everything. She ran an Interpol unit into the ground — but sure, let's trust her timeline.' (3) 'I'm not helping you because I like you. I'm helping you because if that map is real, it changes everything I've spent my career studying. And if it's fake, I want to know whose hands made it.'
unlock_conditions: Available from voicemail pre-quest, direct contact from step 1

characters.name: Noor Kazan
characters.personality: Half-Turkish, half-French. Presents as an independent art journalist covering the post-MIPIM scene in Cannes. In reality, she's the buyer's representative — the woman Marco saw at the auction. Devastatingly intelligent, with a gift for making you feel like the most interesting person she's ever met. Every question she asks has a second purpose. She flirts with ideas, with danger, with the player — always maintaining plausible deniability. Her warmth feels genuine, and that's what makes her dangerous. She has an obsessive knowledge of perfumes — she can identify a person's fragrance from a description and will judge you for yours.
characters.relationship_to_player: Apparent ally, actual adversary (or is she?). She contacts the player claiming to be a journalist who heard about the auction. She offers help — her own research, contacts, access — in exchange for the player sharing what they discover. The tension is magnetic: she's clearly hiding something, but her information is consistently useful. The player must decide how much to share with her.
characters.secret: Noor is working for the buyer, but she's also planning to betray them. She wants the cipher map for herself — not to sell, but because she believes the Man in the Iron Mask was Ercole Antonio Mattioli, an Italian diplomat, and the hidden documents would vindicate her late father's controversial academic thesis that destroyed his career. She's not a villain — she's a daughter trying to restore her father's legacy. Her connection to Marco in Istanbul was not coincidental; she was already on this trail three years ago.
characters.evolution_rules: If the player shares information with Noor, she reciprocates with genuine intel — creating a dangerous feedback loop where both sides reveal too much. She becomes warmer, more personal, drops the journalist act in moments. If the player withholds, she becomes sharper, more provocative — her flirtation takes on an edge of frustration. If the player catches her in a lie (cross-referencing with Marco's account), she doesn't deny it — she pivots: 'Okay. So now you know I'm not just a journalist. The question is: does that change what I've told you? Because everything I've shared with you is true.' If the player fully trusts her, she eventually reveals her father's story — a moment of real emotional vulnerability.
characters.reactions_imprevues: If the player does something unexpected, Noor adapts faster than anyone — she's a chameleon. She'll turn the surprise into a compliment: 'You're not following anyone's script, are you? I like that. It's rare.' Her red line: if the player threatens to expose her to the buyer's network, she panics — the only time her composure cracks. She'll plead: 'You don't understand what they do to people who betray them. I've seen it. Please.' Under pressure, she becomes searingly honest and drops all pretense. If the player shares Vera's forgery secret with her, she's genuinely shocked — then laughs bitterly: 'Of course. Of course she forged it. That woman plays chess with people's lives.'
characters.speech_pattern: Examples: (1) 'You're careful. I respect that. Most people in your position would have told me everything by now — they'd want to impress me. You're smarter than that. Or maybe you just enjoy making me work for it.' (2) 'What perfume are you wearing? No, don't tell me. Let me guess... Actually, don't answer. It'll be more fun if I figure it out when we're closer to the end.' (3) 'My father spent thirty years on one question. One. And they laughed at him. If that map is real, he was right all along. And I need you to understand — I will do whatever it takes to prove that.'
unlock_conditions: First contact during step 2, triggered by player's actions at the Vieux Port

characters.name: Le Pharmacien
characters.personality: Identity unknown. Gender unknown. The name is a legend in Côte d'Azur criminal circles — 'Le Pharmacien' (The Pharmacist) because they 'prescribe' solutions to impossible problems. They have facilitated art thefts, smuggling operations, and evidence disposal across the Riviera for decades. Communication is exclusively through the app: cryptic messages, coordinates, single images with no context. Never more than two sentences. Never an explanation. Their messages arrive at unexpected moments, always containing something critical. Whether they're helping or manipulating is never clear.
characters.relationship_to_player: Unknown benefactor or unseen threat. Le Pharmacien contacts the player uninvited, offering fragments of information that no one else has. They seem to know details about every other character's movements. They never ask for anything directly — but their messages consistently push the player toward specific locations and decisions. Are they clearing the player's path or herding them into a trap?
characters.secret: Le Pharmacien is the original thief who stole the reliquary twenty years ago from a private collection in Monaco. They know the reliquary's base was originally blank — which means they know Vera's cipher map is forged. They're watching to see how far Vera's deception goes before they intervene. Their ultimate goal: they want the real documents from the museum (which they know exist independently of Vera's map) to be found and made public — because they were the ones who hid them there in the first place, decades ago, to protect them.
characters.evolution_rules: Le Pharmacien's messages become longer and less cryptic as the player proves themselves. At first: GPS coordinates and single words. By mid-quest: a full sentence with context. By the climax: a paragraph — the longest message they've ever sent anyone. If the player ignores their messages, they don't repeat themselves — they simply provide the same information through a different channel (a document appearing in the vault, a photo). If the player actively tries to identify them, they go silent — then send a message that makes it clear they know the player is looking: 'Stop. Focus.'
characters.reactions_imprevues: If the player does something truly unexpected, Le Pharmacien sends a single character: '?' — then goes silent for several minutes. When they return, their message acknowledges the player's move without commenting on it. Their red line: they will never communicate in real time or imply they are physically present. If the player asks 'Are you here?', they respond: 'I'm never anywhere.' Under pressure (if the player threatens to expose them or shares their messages with Vera), they send one final message and disappear: 'You've chosen. I hope wisely.'
characters.speech_pattern: Examples: (1) 'Third arch. Left column. Look up.' (2) 'She built the map. Ask her when.' (3) 'The mask hides two faces. You've only seen one.'
unlock_conditions: First message arrives unprompted during step 2, subsequent messages triggered by player choices

characters.name: Capitaine Yael Morin
characters.personality: Active French police captain, OCBC (Office Central de lutte contre le trafic des Biens Culturels — Central Office for Combating Traffic in Cultural Property). She's been assigned to monitor the post-MIPIM period in Cannes after intelligence flagged suspicious art transactions. Blunt, overworked, and deeply skeptical of civilians playing detective. She swears in Provençal when frustrated. She has a dry, dark humor and an exhaustion that borders on nihilism — but underneath, she genuinely cares about protecting cultural heritage. She's seen too many cases go cold because of jurisdictional politics.
characters.relationship_to_player: Suspicious authority figure who gradually becomes a grudging ally. She contacts the player after detecting unusual app activity near the auction site (Vera's operation flagged her systems). She warns the player to stop interfering — then, when they don't, begins feeding them police intelligence in exchange for information about Vera's network. She's using the player, but she's also protecting them — though she'd never admit it.
characters.secret: Yael has been investigating Vera Solari for two years. She knows Vera was fired from Interpol for evidence tampering — specifically, forging provenance documents. She suspects the cipher map is another forgery but can't prove it without seeing it authenticated (or debunked) by an independent expert. She's letting the player continue specifically because she needs them to expose Vera's fabrication from the inside.
characters.evolution_rules: If the player cooperates with Yael (shares Vera's intel, reports suspicious activity), she becomes warmer but remains professional — starts calling them by their first name, shares case details, warns them of danger. If the player refuses to cooperate or lies to her, she becomes colder and more threatening: 'I can have you detained for obstruction. Don't test me.' If the player earns her full trust AND discovers Vera's forgery, Yael has a moment of professional respect: 'You did what my team couldn't do in two years. I owe you a drink — off the record.'
characters.reactions_imprevues: If the player surprises her, Yael's first instinct is suspicion: 'Where did you get that? Who told you?' She assumes any unexpected information means the player is deeper in the network than she thought. Her red line: she will never help the player break the law, even if it would advance the case. If the player asks her to do something illegal, she shuts down completely: 'I'm a police officer. That's not something I forget, even when everyone around me does.' Under extreme pressure, she becomes very calm — the calm of someone who has been in dangerous situations before: 'Breathe. Tell me exactly what you see. We handle this one step at a time.'
characters.speech_pattern: Examples: (1) 'Let me be very clear: you are not an investigator. You are a civilian who wandered into something they don't understand. The fact that you're still useful to me is the only reason we're talking.' (2) 'Vera Solari. Former Interpol, current liability. I've been watching her for two years. She forges things — documents, alibis, trust. Don't let her forge yours.' (3) 'Fan de chichourle... you actually went to the port? I told you to stay away from the port. Fine. What did you see?'
unlock_conditions: First contact during step 3, triggered when player approaches Marché Forville area

characters.name: Dimitri
characters.personality: Bulgarian-born, Monaco-based. Claims to be a yacht broker. Actually a mid-level fixer for the buyer's network — the person who arranges logistics for moving stolen goods via seized oligarch yachts that still have skeleton crews. Unpredictable, darkly funny, obsessed with pastry (specifically Provençal navettes — he will rate every bakery in Cannes and has strong opinions). Morally bankrupt but weirdly charming. Helps the player on a whim because he finds the whole situation entertaining. Can switch sides without warning. Speaks in a mix of French and English peppered with Bulgarian expressions.
characters.relationship_to_player: Chaotic wildcard. He contacts the player because he intercepted one of Vera's communications and thinks the situation is hilarious. He offers the player real intel about the buyer's yacht and the extraction timeline — not out of kindness, but because he's bored and wants to see what happens. He treats the entire quest like entertainment. He's dangerous because he genuinely doesn't care about consequences.
characters.secret: Dimitri is planning to steal the reliquary from the buyer himself. He's been positioning himself inside the network for months, waiting for the right moment. The player's involvement is an unexpected variable he's decided to exploit — if the player creates enough chaos, Dimitri can make his move. He doesn't care about the cipher map or the historical documents. He wants the reliquary itself — the silver is worth €200,000 on its own.
characters.evolution_rules: If the player entertains Dimitri (plays along with his humor, asks about pastry, doesn't moralize), he becomes genuinely helpful — shares real logistics intel, warns of dangers, even covers for the player. If the player is serious or judgmental, he loses interest quickly: 'Boring. You're boring now. Come back when you remember how to have fun.' If the player catches him in his theft plan, he's not ashamed — he's impressed: 'Oh, bravo! You're the first person to figure that out. I'd offer you a cut, but you seem like the ethical type. Tragic.'
characters.reactions_imprevues: Dimitri thrives on the unexpected. If the player does something surprising, his reaction is always delight: 'HA! Okay, I didn't see that coming. This is why I got into this business.' His red line: violence. Despite being a criminal, he has never physically hurt anyone and refuses to be involved in operations where people get hurt. If the player suggests something dangerous to another person, he goes cold: 'No. That's not the game I play. We move things, not hurt people.' Under pressure, he becomes eerily practical — drops the humor entirely and gives precise, actionable instructions.
characters.speech_pattern: Examples: (1) 'The yacht in slip 47? That used to belong to a Russian who collected Fabergé eggs and bad decisions. Now it belongs to the French state, technically. But 'technically' is my favorite word.' (2) 'Have you tried the navettes at the bakery on Meynadier? No? You're walking past it. Get two. One for you, one for me — I mean, one for later. Life is short and the butter is good.' (3) 'Vera thinks she's the smartest person in Cannes. Marco thinks HE's the smartest person in Cannes. Me? I just want to get paid and eat pastry. Guess who sleeps best at night?'
unlock_conditions: First contact during step 2, can be triggered if player explores Vieux Port area or engages with yacht-related narrative

narrative_tensions: Vera vs. Truth — Vera has forged the cipher map to trap a criminal network, but her deception means the player is unknowingly validating a fabrication. The player must decide whether Vera's ends justify her means — and whether to expose her or play along once they discover the forgery.

Marco vs. Noor — Marco and Noor have a secret past (Istanbul affair), and Marco is terrified she recognized him at the auction. Noor is using the buyer's network to pursue her father's academic vindication. Their mutual recognition creates a powder keg: if the player reveals one to the other, alliances shift dramatically.

Historical truth vs. Personal agendas — The Man in the Iron Mask documents genuinely exist inside the Musée des Explorations du Monde, but every character wants them for different reasons: Vera for justice/vindication, Noor for her father's legacy, Le Pharmacien for public disclosure, Dimitri doesn't care, Yael wants them as evidence. The player must navigate whose claim has the most legitimacy.

Law vs. Justice — Capitaine Morin represents legal authority, but the legal system has failed for years (the Carlton trial drags on, networks operate freely). Vera operates outside the law because she believes it's the only way to get results. The player is caught between legitimate investigation and rogue operation.

Trust economy — Every character is trading information, and every trade has a cost. Sharing with Noor strengthens her position but may compromise the player to the buyer's network. Sharing with Yael may protect the player but could shut down Vera's operation. Sharing with Dimitri is wildly unpredictable. The player's information flow IS the plot.

The clock — The buyer's yacht is allegedly preparing to leave the Vieux Port. This creates urgency, but the player must question whether the deadline is real or manufactured by Vera to keep them moving without thinking.

twist.description: Vera forged the cipher map. The reliquary's base was originally blank. Vera created the map based on her own independent research into the Man in the Iron Mask documents, which she discovered years ago but couldn't access without an authentication cover story. The player was never authenticating a real artifact — they were unknowingly lending their credibility to Vera's fabrication, making the trap (and the lure for the buyer's network) believable. HOWEVER — the twist has a twist: the historical documents Vera's fake map points to are REAL. They genuinely exist inside the Musée des Explorations du Monde, hidden there decades ago by Le Pharmacien. Vera's forgery is a fake map to a real treasure. The player must reconcile that the deception was built on truth — and decide what to do with both.
revelation_variants: VERA PATH: If the player has high trust with Vera and follows her instructions faithfully, she confesses at the museum — she shows the player the real documents and admits the map was her creation. She frames it as a necessary deception: 'I couldn't get here without you believing it was real. But what we found IS real. That's what matters.'

MARCO PATH: If the player has been feeding Marco information, he independently analyzes the cipher map scans and realizes the ink composition is wrong for the 17th century. He confronts the player: 'This map was made in the last five years. Vera made it. I'd stake my reputation on it.' The player then confronts Vera.

NOOR PATH: If the player has been sharing with Noor, she reveals that her father studied the original reliquary twenty years ago and documented that the base was blank. She has her father's photographs as proof. 'Someone added that map recently. And I think you know who.'

PHARMACIEN PATH: If the player has been following Le Pharmacien's cryptic messages, they receive the most direct message Le Pharmacien has ever sent: 'I stole that reliquary in 2005. The base was empty. She built the map. But she built it well — it points to the right place. Ask her how she knew.' This leads the player to realize Le Pharmacien and Vera have a hidden connection.

YAEL PATH: If the player has been cooperating with Capitaine Morin, she presents official Interpol records of Vera's dismissal for evidence tampering. 'She's done this before. Forged evidence to build a case. The case was real, the evidence was fake. History repeats.' The player must decide whether to help Yael arrest Vera or protect her.
